questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An illuminated object is positioned on the principal axis of a converging lens such that a real image is produced on the opposite side. If the object is displaced by a small amount,
options: the image shifts a little later than the object
the image shifts a little earlier than the object
the image shifts simultaneously with the object
questions.solution:
The speed of light is finite. When the object is displaced, the light rays from the new position of the object take a finite amount of time to travel to the lens and then to the image plane.

Due to this finite propagation time, the information about the object's displacement reaches the image location after a small time delay. Therefore, the image shifts a little later than the object.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Light travels through a closed cylindrical tube filled with a gas. As the gas is gradually pumped out, the speed of light inside the tube will
questions.solution:
The speed of light in a medium is given by $v = \dfrac{c}{\mu}$, where $c$ is the speed of light in vacuum and $\mu$ is the refractive index of the medium.

The refractive index of a gas is slightly greater than $1$.

As the gas is gradually pumped out, the density of the gas decreases, causing the medium inside the tube to approach a vacuum. Consequently, the refractive index $\mu$ of the gas decreases towards $1$.

Since the refractive index $\mu$ decreases, the speed of light $v$ inside the tube increases.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The speeds of red light and yellow light are exactly equal
options: in air but not in vacuum
in vacuum but not in air
questions.solution:
In vacuum, all electromagnetic waves travel with the same speed $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$, regardless of their wavelength. Thus, the speeds of red and yellow light are exactly equal in vacuum.

In a material medium like air, the speed of light is given by $v = \dfrac{c}{\mu}$, where $\mu$ is the refractive index of the medium. According to Cauchy's relation, the refractive index depends on the wavelength as $\mu = A + \dfrac{B}{\lambda^2}$. Since the wavelength of red light is greater than that of yellow light ($\lambda_{\text{red}} > \lambda_{\text{yellow}}$), the refractive index of air for red light is slightly less than that for yellow light ($\mu_{\text{red}} < \mu_{\text{yellow}}$).

Therefore, the speed of red light in air is slightly greater than the speed of yellow light ($v_{\text{red}} > v_{\text{yellow}}$). The speeds are exactly equal in vacuum but not in air.
